---
format: html
editor: visual
---

We selected `r length(unique(detect_data$NWFSCsampleID))` water samples collected at `r length(unique(detect_data$station))` unique locations during the 2019 NOAA hake abundance survey (@fig-bubble), targeting 0, 50, 150, 300, and 500 m sampling depths at each station. Where the bottom was less than 500 m deep, we did not collect a sample at 500 m. We amplified and sequenced these samples in 1 technical replicate using MFU, 3 technical replicates using MV1, and 3-5 technical replicates using DL, resulting in a final total of `r detect_data %>% group_by(primer, NWFSCsampleID) %>% n_groups()` amplified and sequenced samples.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sp-detects
#| tbl-cap: "Scientific names, common names, abbrevation, taxonomic family, broad prey category, and total number of detections for each cetacean species detected in our study area."

table <- mmEcoEvo %>% 
  select(Species, common_name, abbrev, Family, Broad_taxa, Prey.family) %>% 
  inner_join(detect_per_species, by = c("Species" = "BestTaxon")) %>% 
  arrange(desc(nDetect)) %>% 
  rename("# Detections" = nDetect, "Common name" = common_name, "Sp. abbrevation" = abbrev, "Prey family" = Prey.family, "Broad taxon" = Broad_taxa) %>% 
  left_join(detect_per_primer_species, by = c("Species" = "BestTaxon"))

pander(table)
```

Across all samples, we observed `r sum(detect_data$Detected)` positive detections of `r length(unique(detect_data$BestTaxon))` cetacean species, including 6 baleen whale species, 5 dolphin species, 2 porpoise species, and 3 beaked whale species (@tbl-sp-detects). `r nrow(detect_per_species %>% filter(nDetect > 10))` species were observed more than 10 times, and and additional `r nrow(detect_per_species %>% filter(nDetect < 10))` were observed fewer than 10 times. This includes one detection of a bowhead whale (*Balaena mysticetus*), as well as three detections of saber-toothed whales (*Mesoplodon stejnegeri*). @fig-bubble illustrates the distribution of detections across sampling depths, ignoring x-y distribution, for all cetacean species included in the study.

```{r}
#| label: fig-bubble
#| fig-cap: "Left: Distribution of detections across sampling depths for all species included in the study. Species are grouped as follows: Baleen whales includes the Balaenopteridae, Balaenidae, and Eschericthidae; Dolphins/Porpoises includes all Delphinidae and Phocoenidae; Beaked whales includes all Ziphiidae. Right: Geographic distribution of samples used in this study, acquired from water collected during a 2019 survey of hake distribution in the California Current. "
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6

aligned <- align_plots(detectDepth_bubble, map, align = "v", axis = "tblr")
plot_grid(plotlist = aligned, ncol = 2, rel_widths = c(3, 1))
```

### Question 1: Does POD vary with depth?

GAM-based model selection of detection probability across sampling depths negated the null hypothesis of no variation across depth (Supplemental Figure S1). The best alternative model includes significant smooth terms for depth, species, and depth x species interaction (AIC = `r round(min(modelAIC$AIC))`, @tbl-modelAIC). This model explained 14.3% of variance in eDNA detection probability, with a mean AUC of `r round(mean(AUC_df$AUC), digits = 2)` and a mean optimized TSS of `r round(mean(TSS_optim$TSS), digits = 2)`. Full model TSS for all thresholds, as well as per-species AUC, optimized TSS, TSS values across all thresholds are shown in Supplemental Table S2 and Supplemental Figure S2.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-modelAIC
#| tbl-cap: "Akaike Information Criterion values for all GAMs tested to characterize the relationship between depth and POD of eDNA."

modelAICtable <- modelAIC %>% 
  arrange(AIC) %>% 
  mutate(minAIC = min(AIC)) %>% 
  mutate(deltaAIC = AIC-minAIC) %>% 
  mutate("stratification" = c("species", "species", "family", "prey", "none")) %>% 
  mutate(model = case_when(model == "m1.2"~"depth, s(species)",
                           model == "m1.1"~"depth + species",
                           model == "m1.2a"~"depth, s(family)",
                           model == "m1.2b"~"depth, s(prey type)",
                           model == "m1.0"~"depth", 
                           TRUE~model)) %>% 
  select(model, stratification, df, deltaAIC)

pander(modelAICtable)
```

Broadly, delphinids were more likely to be detected at the surface, beaked whales at depths \> 200m, and baleen whales either at the surface or at depths \> 400m (@fig-H1POD-species). While detection data from the five species detected fewer than 10 times were included in our model input, we did not make predictions for any of these species. Species-specific model predictions generally agreed with the raw detection data.

Among the delphinids, species-specific trends in POD generally reflected model estimates of time-at-depth. Conversely, most beaked whale and baleen whale species in the study were most likely to be detected deeper than where they spend most of their time (@fig-H1POD-species). Most notably, predicted POD-at-depth plots for two of the three baleen whales indicated a high probability of detection at 500 m, while these two species spend most of their time above \~200 m.

```{r}
#| label: fig-H1POD-species
#| fig-cap: "Species-specific probability of detection across all depths sampled in this study. Solid line shows POD; shaded regions show 95%, 75%, and 50% confidence intervals. Dashed line shows proportional time at depth. Species abbreviation codes can be found in Table 1; plots are colored by taxonomic category as outlined in Table 1."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

m1.2POD

```

Models stratifying detections by taxonomic Family and preferred prey type both had lower AIC values than the model stratified by species (AIC = `r round(filter(modelAIC, model == "m1.2a")$AIC, digits = 0)` and `r round(filter(modelAIC, model == "m1.2b")$AIC, digits = 0)`, respectively); however, these models still had significant terms for Family and depth x Family interactions (taxonomic model) and for depth, prey type, and depth x prey type interactions (prey type model). *Model diagnostics here.* Predicted POD curves for each model illustrate varying trends in POD with depth across strata (@fig-H1POD-family-prey). Similar to the model stratified by species, we did not make predictions for Families with fewer than 10 detections using the taxonomic model, which excluded the Balaenidae (bowhead whale family).

```{r}
#| label: fig-H1POD-family-prey
#| fig-cap: "Left: POD by depth for species stratified by taxonomic Family. Right: POD by depth for species stratified based on broad prey categories."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

m1.2aPOD_bordered <- patchwork::wrap_elements(m1.2aPOD) +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 1))

m1.2bPOD_bordered <- patchwork::wrap_elements(m1.2bPOD) +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 1))

(m1.2aPOD_bordered + m1.2bPOD_bordered) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(3, 1), axis_titles = "collect") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

```

#### Question 2: Does depth variability in POD covary with observation bias?

Our hierarchical model indicates that given the presence of a cetacean species' eDNA in a sample, the probability of detecting that DNA in downstream processing is affected by primer specificity, DNA degradation by freeze/thaw cycles, and the number of technical replicates used to used to probe the sample (@fig-H2POD-obsbias). Sequencing runs using MV1 and MFU primers, which target regions of XX and XX bp in length, were not affected by the number of freeze-thaw cycles accumulated prior to sequencing. Sequencing runs using the DL primer set, which targets a region of XX bp in length, was affected by freeze thaw cycles such that sequencing runs with 1 freeze/thaw cycle had a higher detection rate than MV1 or MFU and sequencing runs with 2-XX freeze/thaw cycles had a lower detection rate thn MV1 or MFU.

```{r}
#| label: fig-H2POD-obsbias
#| fig-cap: "Primer-specific detection probability for each primer used in this study. MFU = Mifish, MV1 = MarVer1, DL = Dloop (1 freeze/thaw cycle, DL2 = Dloop (2+ freeze/thaw cycles)."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

knitr::include_graphics(c("./Figures/detectThaw.png", "./Figures/m1.0+2LevelOcc_PDetection.png"))


```

#### Question 3: Does depth variability in POD covary with xy variability?

Our best fit model included terms for species, lat/lon, and depth, as well as species x lat/lon, species x depth, and species x depth x lat/lon (AIC = `r AIC(m3.0c)`), and explained 25.3% of the variability in eDNA detection. Although terms for depth and lat/lon were not significant, removing these terms increased model AIC to 4947. *Model AUC and TSS here*

Predicted vertical probability of detection for the most frequently detected delphinid, baleen whale, and beaked whale varied with latitute and longitude, while adhering to the broad trends identified in Q1 (@fig-H3PODxy). The Pacific white-side dolphin is most likely to be detected near the surface, with max POD varying between depths of `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Lagenorhynchus obliquidens") %>% pull(depth))` m and `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Lagenorhynchus obliquidens") %>% pull(depth))` m. Generally, detections of Pacific white-sided dolphins that were farther south and east were also more likely to be deeper in the water column.

Depths of highest POD for humpback whales had two peaks around `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Megaptera novaeangliae") %>% pull(depth))` m and `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Megaptera novaeangliae") %>% pull(depth))` m; and depths of highest POD for Baird's beaked whales varied between `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Berardius bairdii") %>% pull(depth))` m and `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Berardius bairdii") %>% pull(depth))` m. Lat/lon variability in Baird's beaked whale detections followed a similar pattern to Pacific white-sided dolphins, with greater likelihood of deeper detections in stations that are more to the south and east. The two peaks in depth of greatest POD for humpback whales were roughly distributed with higher probability of detection around 0 m on the continental shelf, and higher probability of detection around 500 m on the shelf break.

```{r}
#| label: fig-H3PODxy
#| fig-cap: "Depth of maximum probability of detection (POD; top) and range of depths falling within 50% CI of the depth of maximum detection (bottom) for three species: Baird's beaked whale (*B. bairdii*), max POD ~ 0.018; Pacific white-sided dolphin (*L. obliquidens*), max POD ~ 0.08; humpback whale (*M. novaegliae*), max POD ~ 0.04. Regions with max POD < 0.3% were masked."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 15

depth_max_detect / ci95_plot

```

In addition to examining geographic patterns in the depth of maximum POD, we examined uncertainty around these values by mapping the range of depths that fell within the 50% confidence interval of maximum POD (@fig-H3PODxy, bottom). In the northern portion of the study area, where Pacific white-sided dolphins are most likely to be observed near the surface, the range of depths within the 50% CI of maximum POD is small (minimum depth range = `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Lagenorhynchus obliquidens") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m). That range increases toward the south and east (maximum depth range = `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Lagenorhynchus obliquidens") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m), as the depth of maximum POD gets deeper for this species. Baird's beaked whales exhibited the opposite trend: the range of depths falling within the 50% CI of maximum POD was smallest in the southest portion of the range (minimum depth range = `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Berardius bairdii") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m), where the depth of maximum POD was deepest, and increased in the northern part of the range (maximum depth range = `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Berardius bairdii") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m). The bimodal peak of maximum POD observed in humpback whale eDNA was mirrored in the range of depths within the 50% CI of maximum POD: the depth range was largest in the transition zones between the two peaks (maximum depth range = `r max(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Megaptera novaeangliae") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m), and relatively small outside of the transition zones (minimum depth range = `r min(maxPOD_depth_clipped %>% filter(BestTaxon == "Megaptera novaeangliae") %>% pull(depthWidth))` m).
